frameworks.name: Consumable Input Framework
frameworks.definition: A community offer has two separable components: (1) a one-time method/knowledge transfer and (2) recurring consumable inputs that the community must refresh each period. Retention and recurring revenue attach to the consumable layer, not to the knowledge layer.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when information is infinite and freely available, the economic value shifts entirely to curation and selection — not to information itself — because no individual has the time to identify what is good from the full corpus. The consultant who charges for handing someone a cupped scoop of water from a river is not being paid for the water but for the selection act.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: When supply is infinite, the binding constraint shifts to attention and filtering capacity; the scarce resource becomes the act of identifying what is worth consuming, not the raw content itself.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that information has been effectively free since the rise of the public internet, well before AI — AI did not create the commoditization problem, it accelerated an already-existing dynamic. The apparent novelty of AI-powered free information is therefore not a new strategic challenge but a continuation of the same challenge creators faced with search engines.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the underlying value most creators and educators were always delivering is time compression — reducing the time a customer would need to find, test, and distill information on their own. When a customer pays for a course or coaching, they are paying to skip years of trial-and-error, not to access facts that are already publicly available.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that in a world where AI can generate polished graphics, ebooks, and generic content at near-zero cost, low-proof creators whose differentiation rested on production polish (e.g., making a beautiful ebook or pretty graphics) lose their edge entirely, because that capability is now commoditized for any competitor.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Creator's differentiation was production quality — graphics, PDF aesthetics, template design — not a unique method or real-world result
claims.mechanism: AI can replicate visual production at zero marginal cost, eliminating the supply constraint that made that skill valuable.
bounded_by: Does not apply to creators whose differentiation is a proprietary method, a community, or documented real-world results that AI cannot fabricate

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that community is an inherently consumable product — a member who benefited from community last month still wants community this month — whereas one-time educational content (a course, a webinar) delivers its full value in a single consumption event and therefore cannot anchor ongoing retention.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Community delivers belonging, accountability, and fresh peer interaction each period; these benefits reset and renew rather than depleting after the first exposure.
bounded_by: One-time components such as a core method or process document have residual value but cannot sustain month-over-month retention by themselves

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a community offer has two structurally distinct components: (1) a one-time knowledge layer — the founder's method, process, or worldview — and (2) a recurring consumable input layer — the specific, time-sensitive raw materials that members need to apply that method each period. Retention and recurring revenue attach primarily to the consumable layer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Offer includes a teachable method or framework plus ongoing application inputs
claims.mechanism: The consumable input (ads, trending products, pre-vetted deals, stock picks) expires after each period, creating a structural demand for renewal that the one-time knowledge layer cannot replicate.
bounded_by: Applies to communities with a practice-based method whose inputs are perishable or time-sensitive; does not apply where the core value is a one-time credential or certification

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that as AI-generated content proliferates, people will increasingly crave authentic human realness — real people going through real struggles — and that community platforms built around genuine human interaction will therefore grow in value rather than decline.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: In a media environment where AI can generate convincing synthetic content at scale
claims.mechanism: Scarcity of authentic human expression increases its perceived value as AI makes synthetic content abundant; the counterfactual (overwhelming AI content) raises the premium on anything verifiably human.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that people are already more lonely in the age of social media — a technology designed ostensibly to connect people — and that this loneliness gap signals a growing unmet demand for genuine human community that AI and social platforms cannot fill.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that one-time educational products (courses, webinars) must be priced relative to the one-time value they deliver, and that attempting to sustain membership-level retention with one-time content will fail because the value extraction happens immediately and does not renew.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Membership or community business model where ongoing retention is the goal

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that removing friction from people's lives — making things faster or easier — is a durable source of value that AI does not eliminate, because humans will always aggregate toward lower-friction paths regardless of the technology that delivers the friction reduction.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Human cognitive load creates persistent demand for friction reduction; this demand is invariant to the technology layer providing it.

examples.text: Alex Hormozi described Gym Launch as an example of the consumable input model: the core community taught members how to run their gym and sell memberships, but the recurring consumable input was ads. Gym Launch's team would test ads, identify winners, and deliver those winners to members each period. Because ad creatives go stale and new campaigns are constantly needed, the input refreshed monthly, anchoring retention.

Hormozi described a 3D printing community whose founder had taught members his print methodology once. The recurring consumable input was a weekly or monthly list of trending products worth printing. Members consumed the method once but returned each period for fresh product picks, because last month's trending products were no longer actionable.

Hormozi cited a wholesale real estate community as a high-retention example: the founder's team pre-screened properties, ran the numbers, and delivered a list of vetted deals each week. Members who had already learned the evaluation method still needed fresh deal flow every week, because last week's deals were either gone or off-market. The consumable (vetted deal list) renewed the reason to stay subscribed.

Hormozi mentioned financial communities that deliver stock picks as the recurring consumable input. The investment method or framework is taught once; the specific actionable picks are generated weekly or monthly and consumed immediately, making the ongoing subscription necessary for anyone who wants to apply the method.

Hormozi referenced a community member whose differentiation used to be creating beautiful ebooks or polished graphics — a genuine skill that gave an edge when design tools were difficult to use. With AI now generating comparable quality graphics or PDFs on demand, that edge has been erased entirely. The example illustrates why production-polish-based differentiation collapses when AI commoditizes the production step.

practices.text: Identify the consumable portion of your community's value separately from the one-time method or knowledge transfer, then price recurring membership against the consumable component only.
If you sell courses or webinars as one-time products, price them relative to the one-time value delivered; do not expect one-time content to sustain ongoing membership retention.
